coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived carbon dots mediated exciton dissociation in rose flower-like carbon nitride for boosting photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Ke
Shao, Weifan
Li, Haitong
Guo, Wen
Bian, Huiyang
Han, Jiangang
Wu, Guangyu
Xing, Weinan
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable valorization of forestry waste products as substrate materials to realize energy conversion and environmental purification via the photocatalysis technology holds great promise due to its low cost, abundance and sustainability. The metal-free photocatalyst graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is known for its low toxicity, high stability, favorable optical properties, and controllable structure. Whereas, the strong exciton effects by Coulomb interactions between excited electrons and holes severely impede the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. Herein, by introducing biomass carbon quantum dots (prepared from ginkgo leaves, BQ) in rose flower-like carbon nitride (FCN), it was demonstrated that the construction heterojunction between BQ and FCN can not only facilitate the dissociation of excitons and the transfer of charge, but also enhance light absorption. As a result, in comparison with CN, the optimized zero dimension-three dimensions (0D–3D) BQ-FCN displayed much better photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production rate (3400 μmol h−1 g−1) as well as better degradation performance of Rhodamine B (RhB), which is much higher than those carbon decorated CN and 3D CN photocatalysts in previous reports. It is believed that this work provides a meaningful guidance in using forestry waste materials to adjust the dissociation of excitons, and makes a significant process in the development of metal-free and highly efficient photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon quantum dots
Graphitic carbon nitride
Photocatalysis
H2 evolution
Organic dyes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of biomass derived carbon quantum dots (BQ)
Preparation of rose flower-like CN modification with BQ (BQ- FCN) heterojunction photocatalytic
Exciton dissociation and charge transfer processes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21901119 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of BQ (a) and BQ-FCN10 sample (b, c, d).
XRD patterns (a), and FTIR spectra (b) of different samples.
XPS spectra (a), High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c) and O 1 s (d) of CN and BQ-FCN10.
UV–vis DRS (a) (αhν)1/2 versus hν (b), XPS valence band spectra (c) Mott–Schottky plots (d-e) and schematic illustration of the band structure (e) of different samples.
PL spectra (a), TRPL spectra (b), and photocurrent responses (c) of different samples.
Photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of different samples (a), stability of BQ-FCN10 over four consecutive runs (b) and comparison with other CN-based photocatalysts.
Degradation curves (a), graphic of ln(C0/C) versus reaction time for the RhB (b), and radical trapping experiments of BQ-FCN10 (c).
Photocatalytic mechanism of BQ-FCN10 under the illumination of visible light.
Schematic illustration of experimental flow over BQ-FCN.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived carbon dots mediated exciton dissociation in rose flower-like carbon nitride for boosting photocatalytic performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biology and the Environment, Co-Innovation Center for the Sustainable Forestry in Southern China, Nanjing Forestry University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable valorization of forestry waste products as substrate materials to realize energy conversion and environmental purification via the photocatalysis technology holds great promise due to its low cost, abundance and sustainability. The metal-free photocatalyst graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is known for its low toxicity, high stability, favorable optical properties, and controllable structure. Whereas, the strong exciton effects by Coulomb interactions between excited electrons and holes severely impede the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. Herein, by introducing biomass carbon quantum dots (prepared from ginkgo leaves, BQ) in rose flower-like carbon nitride (FCN), it was demonstrated that the construction heterojunction between BQ and FCN can not only facilitate the dissociation of excitons and the transfer of charge, but also enhance light absorption. As a result, in comparison with CN, the optimized zero dimension-three dimensions (0D–3D) BQ-FCN displayed much better photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production rate (3400 μmol h−1 g−1) as well as better degradation performance of Rhodamine B (RhB), which is much higher than those carbon decorated CN and 3D CN photocatalysts in previous reports. It is believed that this work provides a meaningful guidance in using forestry waste materials to adjust the dissociation of excitons, and makes a significant process in the development of metal-free and highly efficient photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid industrialization process aggravates the energy crisis and environmental pollution. Photocatalysis technology is recognized as one of the most promising ways to resolve the above problems. As a green, economic and effective technology, photocatalysis is not only able to turn solar power into chemical energy, but able to degrade pollutants under the ambient operating temperature and pressure (Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019). Consequently, a variety of photocatalysts have been developed, such as metal oxides, metal sulfides, nitrogen oxides, covalent organic framework, and carbon nitride, and so on (Chen et al., 2020; Tu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022a,b). Organic semiconductor graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has attracted extensive attention owing to the high chemical stability, unique band structure, metal-free components, and relatively cheap raw material (Wang et al., 2021b; Yang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021b). Unfortunately, the original CN has some disadvantages, for example, low specific surface area, limited light harvesting ability and sluggish transfer and separation ability of photogenerated carriers (Xing et al., 2022b; Zhao et al., 2021a). Particularly, the photoinduced excitons in CN prefer to recombine because of the strong Coulombic interaction between excited electrons and holes. To solve these issues, various methods have been explored, element doping, crystal-structure engineering, construction of heterojunction, and morphological design et al. (Che et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021a; Xu et al., 2021).

Considering that the exciton is the bound of electron and hole, it would be reasonable to gain considerable charge carriers by dissociation of exciton (Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021c; Wang et al., 2017). Recently, Xie’s group proposed that the excitons in CN were more likely to dissociate at the interface between black phosphorous and polymeric CN heterojunction (Li et al., 2021c). Sun et al. built a black phosphorus quantum dots/TiO2 heterojunction (Guan et al., 2022). The resulting energy difference between the conduction bands turned out to be the driving force for exciton dissociation, which increased the photocatalytic activity by 2.4 times. These methods effectively improve the photocatalytic activity from exciton dissociation level, but suffer from the cumbersome steps, hazardous reagents, and high cost of raw materials. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, rapid and low-cost way to regulate the dissociation of exciton.

Compared with conventional semiconductors, carbon-based materials, especially biomass carbon which derives from the natural resources (such as, plant straw, forest residue and stem et al.) has drawn researchers’ attention due to the low cost and sustainable development (Peng et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2020). More importantly, it also provides an ideal way for the high-value utilization of biomass wastes. Fallen leaves from forestry treated as vital biomass resource and renewable resource. Ginkgo trees are often planted along streets or on the campuses, and leaves always fall in autumn and are treated as waste. So, transferring these waste leaves to resources is still a big challenge.

Different from bulk carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have special advantages of quantum size effect and short carrier transport distance, which are quite important in the field of photocatalysis (Liu et al., 2021; Qiu et al., 2022). Huang and cooperators compounded a suites of carbon quantum dots/porous CN heterostructures and demonstrated their efficient photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) evolution (Li et al., 2021b). Yang and co-workers constructed nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots modified sulfur doped carbon nitride S-scheme heterojunction for the photocatalytic H2 production and degradation performance (Li et al., 2022). Obviously, the previous works have revealed that the introduction of CQDs in CN photocatalyst is a good approach to increasing visible light absorption level and facilitating the separation efficiency of charge. Whereas, the studies about the mechanism of biomass carbon quantum dots in regulation exciton dissociation is reported rarely.

In this work, a highly efficient metal-free 0D–3D heterojunctions featuring with biomass carbon quantum dots (prepared from ginkgo leaves, BQ) anchored on the surface of rose flower-like carbon nitride (FCN) were designed. The agricultural or forestry waste (ginkgo leaf) was used as the precursor to prepare well-dispersed BQ. As expected, the 0D–3D BQ-FCN heterojunction displayed much better photocatalytic degradation and H2 evolution performance than that of CN. The effects of BQ decorated on the surface of FCN for the exciton dissociation and charge transfer behaviors were explored with the aid of photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) techniques and photoelectrochemical characterization techniques. Combination with experimental and characteristic analysis, it was proposed that the incorporation of BQ promoted light absorption. What's more, coupling FCN with BQ to form heterojunction effectively facilitated the exciton dissociation in FCN and charge transfer from FCN to BQ. Finally, the reasonable photocatalytic mechanisms and electron transfer process of BQ-FCN heterojunction photocatalyst were proposed in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass derived carbon quantum dots (BQ)
ce:section.ce:para: The BQ in this work were prepared from ginkgo leaves. The mixed solution which contains 0.5 g ginkgo leaf powder and 35 mL deionized water was heated at 200 ℃ for 8 h in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. When the temperature cooled down to room temperature, purified the mixture with dialysis bag to obtain the target BQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of rose flower-like CN modification with BQ (BQ- FCN) heterojunction photocatalytic
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed solution consisted of 1.0 g melamine and 40 mL dimethyl sulfoxide was named as “A solution”. “B solution” is a mixture of 1.0 g cyanuric acid dispersing in 20 mL dimethyl sulfoxide. Both A and B solution were ultrasonicated for 5 min, and stirred on magnetic stirrer for 10 min. Next, still under the condition of stirring, a specific amount of BQ solution was slowly added dropwise into B solution. After the reactants were uniformly mixed, add B solution into A solution drop by drop under the stirring condition continuously. The mixed solution was filtered after 30 min, washed and dried at 60 ℃. Finally, the dried powder (in a crucible) was heated up to 520 ℃ with the temperature increase rate of 2 ℃/min and then kept warm at 520 ℃ for 2 h to obtain the BQ-FCNx heterojunction. According to the amount of BQ, the target samples using different volumes of BQ (500 μL, 1000 μL, and 1500 μL) were named as BQ-FCN5, BQ-FCN10 and BQ-FCN15, respectively. For comparison, the pristine CN was obtained by directly heating melamine and cyanuric acid at the similar procedure without adding BQ. The experimental flow was shown in Scheme 1. The other experimental details such as characterization, photocatalytic activity testing and photoelectrochemical measurements were provided in the ESI.

The morphological characteristics of samples were recorded by TEM images. The TEM image of pure BQ is used for comparison. The monodispersed spherical morphology of BQ with diameter < 10 nm is observed from Fig. 1a. For BQ-FCN10 sample, it exhibits a well-defined rose flower-like spherical. The diameter of BQ-FCN10 sample is about 2–3 µm (Fig. 1b). The rose flower-like structure is built by porous nanosheet units which is well-ordered and directed self-assembly. The evident difference of brightness between the center and edge of BQ-FCN10 suggests the hollow rose flower-like morphology. Meanwhile, the morphology is actually comprised by crumpled nanosheets with numerous wrinkles. The hollow and porous structure of BQ-FCN10 is further confirmed by N2 adsorption–desorption experiments (Fig.S1). The specific surface area of BQ/FCN-10 is 92.48 m2 g−1, and a wide pore size distribution is observed. From the enlarged TEM image in Fig. 1c, some little black spots are spread across the surface of nanosheet and formed close contact between them. Besides, Fig. 1d shows well-defined lattice spacing, which corresponds to BQ, and the BQ is embedded in CN, verifying that the BQ-FCN heterojunction photocatalyst has been successfully constructed.

The crystal structure information was studied by XRD pattern. All samples exhibit similar diffraction characteristics in Fig. 2a, the 13.1° peak is from the in-plane aromatic ring structure units (100) and reduplicative stacking of layers (002) (Yu et al., 2022). Moreover, the (100) peak almost disappears with the increasing of BQ amount in BQ-FCNx (Fig. S2a), which indicates that the well-ordered in-plane structure of g-C3N4 is broken after BQ embedding. The enlarged (002) peak is showed in Fig. S2b, the (002) peak shows a slight shift from 27.7° to 27.4° compared with CN. Based on Bragg’s law, the low angles suggests the increase of interlayer distance, which suggests the strong interaction between BQ and FCN leads to larger interlayer distance in BQ-FCNx (x = 5,10,15). Besides, the depressed diffraction intensity of (002) with the increasing amount of BQ in BQ-FCNx confirms that the reduplicative stacking of layers of FCN are influenced by the π-π conjugate interaction between BQ and FCN (Li et al., 2022). Apart from that, the functional group information of the as-prepared samples was measured by FTIR. From Fig. 2d, the broad bands located in 3200–3500 cm−1 belong to amino groups (-NH) and hydroxyl group (-OH) of the surface-bonded H2O molecules and unreacted amino groups. The absorption bands ranging from 1250 to 1650 cm−1 stand for stretching vibrations of aromatic heterocycles. The peak at 808 cm−1 proves the existence of tri-s-triazine units (Xing et al., 2022a). No obvious variation of the characteristic FTIR suggests that the main skeleton of CN is maintained.

The as-prepared samples’ elemental constitution and surface chemical states were conducted by XPS. The XPS spectra in Fig. 3a, CN and BQ-FCN10 mainly consist of C, N, and O elements. The C 1 s spectra of CN (Fig. 3b) show three peaks. The peak at 284.6 eV represents graphitic carbon’s C-C bonds, and the other two peaks at 286.1 eV and 287.9 eV represent C-NH2 and N-CN, which are from sp2 C atoms of tri-s-triazine rings attached to amino group, and sp2 C atom bonded to N atom in the triazine rings, respectively (Dong et al., 2021). Compared with CN, the peak in 284.6 eV for BQ-FCN10 is stronger, the increased carbon content is from BQ. From Fig. 3c, N 1s spectra can be divided into four peaks, which are 398.2 eV, 399.1 eV, 400.6 eV and 403.8 eV, resulting from sp2 N atoms existing in C-NC bond, N-(C)3 bond, C-N-H bond and positive charge localization in heterocycles, respectively (Lv et al., 2018). Moreover, the N 1s and C 1s spectra of BQ-FCN10 both exhibit evident shift binding energy, which suggest that the BQ could change the surface chemical environment of FCN and interaction between BQ and nanosheet units of FCN (Song et al., 2021). For XPS spectra of O 1s in CN (Fig. 3d), the peak at 532.1 eV would be explained by surface oxidation during the synthesis process or the absorption of water on the surface of samples. In BQ-FCN10, a small new peak centered at 533.6 eV consist with the C-O (as well as CO) groups from the surface hydroxyl groups of BQ (Song et al., 2020). The above results from TEM, XRD, FTIR together with XPS analysis reveal that the BQ have modified on the surface of nanosheet units of FCN and formed strong interaction between BQ and FCN in the BQ-FCN10 sample.

The optical absorption property was measured by UV–vis DRS and showed in Fig. 4a. The absorption edge of CN is appeared at 460 nm. In the case of BQ-FCNx, light harvesting ability obviously enhance across the whole optical spectrum, especially in the region of 450–600 nm, which is attributed to the introduction of BQ in the BQ-FCN10 sample. However, the band gaps of the BQ-FCNx samples have no apparent shift in Fig. 4b. In addition, from Fig. 4c, the valence band (VB) of CN and BQ-FCN10 are located at 1.82 eV and 1.68 eV. According to the Mott–Schottky plots (Fig. 4d, e), the flat-band potentials (Vfb) of CN and BQ-FCN10 are − 1.10, and − 0.88 V. Therefore, the Fermi level (Ef) are calculated to be − 0.49 and − 0.27 eV (Liu et al., 2023, 2022; Mo et al., 2022). Combined with band gaps, XPS VB, and Ef, the band gap diagram of the materials is shown in Fig. 4f. The significant Schottky barrier for electrons to traverse is formed, which leads to a shorter migration path of electrons from FCN to BQ. (Wang et al., 2018). Thereby, the electronic structure of BQ-FCN10 is expected to promote the dissociation of excitons and carrier separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exciton dissociation and charge transfer processes
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) tests were performed to measure the excitonic behavior of the as-prepared samples. It has been reported that the fluorescence is mainly caused by the radiative decay of singlet excitons and the intensity of PL depicts the concentration of singlet excitons (Wang et al., 2017). It can be obviously seen from Fig. 5a that the PL emission peaks of BQ-FCNx are much lower than that of CN, which indicates that the number of singlet excitons decreases after loading BQ. That is to say, the anchoring of BQ on the surface of FCN facilitates the exciton dissociation into free electrons and holes. Additionally, the excitonic behavior was also characteristiced by fluorescent lifetime measurement (Fig. 5b). The decrease of fluorescence lifetime of BQ-FCN10 demonstrates that the BQ promotes excitons dissociation in FCN. The high electronic conductivity of BQ makes the photoinduced electrons easier to transfer, functioning as impetus for excitons dissociation. The interfacial charge transfer behaviors of the as-prepared samples were explored by photoelectrochemistry tests. Compared with CN, BQ-FCN10 (Fig. 5c) shows greater photocurrent density, almost four times the amount of pure CN, which is attributed to the excitons dissociated into free charges and elevating the electron concentration in BQ-FCN10. The better the photocurrent response is, the better charge carriers separation will be (Luo et al., 2022). The enhanced photocurrent response indicates that the BQ-FCN10 has fast separation and transfer ability. Based on these results, it proved that the decoration of BQ on FCN surfaces could elevate the dissociation efficiency of excitons and accelerate charge carriers separation.

With the aim of studying the influence of exciton dissociation on photocatalytic performance, the photocatalytic H2 evolution was first evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6a, CN displays the worst performance with the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 199 μmol h−1 g−1, which can be ascribed to the strong exciton effect along with the recombination of charge carriers. All the BQ decorated FCN samples present the improvement of H2 evolution. Particularly, BQ-FCN10 has the best performance with 3400 μmol h−1g−1, which is over 17 times as high as that of CN. The improved photocatalytic H2 production rate may be owing to the decoration of BQ, which facilitated the exciton dissociation. However, the excess BQ may arouse the light-shield effect, thus reducing the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate. Additionally, in order to test the durability and stability of the photocatalyst, recycle experiment was carried out. In four consecutive runs in Fig. 6b, the H2 amount of BQ-FCN10 could be reached 13,601 μmol g−1 and hardly any loss after four cycles. This result further demonstrates that BQ-FCN10 is a stable photocatalyst in the photocatalytic reaction. Meanwhile, we carefully compare the CN-based photocatalysts reported previously in Fig. 6c and Table S1-S3, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of BQ-FCN10 outperforms most of the previously reported carbon decorated CN and 3D CN photocatalyst.

The degradation performance of Rhodamine B (RhB) is used to evaluate photocatalytic performance under the illumination of visible light. Before the photocatalytic degradation reaction, the absorption properties of RhB over different samples in the dark were also evaluated (Fig. S3). The adsorption-desorption equilibrium of RhB could be reached in 30 min. In Fig. 7a, all of BQ-FCNx samples show better photodegradation performance than CN. In particular, the degradation efficiency of BQ-FCN10 is the best, which can almost completely decompose RhB in 60 min. What’s more, the reaction kinetic of RhB degradation is shown in Fig. 7b, which determined by the graphic of ln (C0/C) vs reaction time. Reading from the fitting results, the optimal rate constant k of BQ-FCN10 (Fig. S4) is 0.078 min−1, which is almost 3.71 times as high as that of CN (0.021 min−1). Furthermore, the circle experiments of RhB degradation (Fig. S5) has no significant loss after five cycles, which further testifies the ideal stability and durability of the as-prepared samples.

Radical trapping experiments of BQ-FCN10 were conducted to find out the major active species during the photocatalytic degradation RhB process. The degradation process is influenced by trapping agent in different degrees (Fig. 7c). Among them, EDTA-2Na and IPA only arouse a small decrease of the photocatalytic activity, implying the h+ and •OH play weak effects on the degradation of RhB (Fig. S6). By contrast, the photocatalytic activity is seriously suppressed when the p-BQ is added in the reaction, which suggests •O2 − is the main reactive species during degradation process. From the ESR spin-trapping spectra in Fig. S7, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-•O2 − are observed and the peak intensity significantly enhances under visible-light irradiation, further suggesting •O2− radicals could be generated and plays an important role in the photocatalytic degradation process (Lei et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023).

According to the investigations above, the possible photocatalytic H2 evolution and degradation mechanism were depicted in Fig. 8. The electrons and holes are generated in FCN under the illumination of visible light. Most of the electron-hole bind with each other together in the form of excitons due to Coulomb interactions (Guo et al., 2021). As revealed by the characterization analyses, the rose flower-like ultrathin nanosheet units of FCN could shorten the exciton diffuse distance from the bulk phase to the surface and more excitons could travel to the surface to participate the reaction. What’s more, the decoration of BQ with high electronic conductivity in FCN is inclined to attract the electrons to promote excitons dissociate into free electrons and holes as well as suppress charge recombination. During the process of photocatalytic H2 evolution, the photogenerated electrons reduce protons to H2 and holes can be sacrificed by TEOA. For the photocatalytic degradation of RhB, the electrons are easily captured by dissolved oxygen to form •O2 − and •O2 − can also react with H+ to form •OH. At last, the generated •O2 −, •OH and h+ active species on the surface of BQ-FCN10 oxidize RhB molecule into small organic (inorganic) molecules (ions) products.

In summary, 0D–3D BQ-FCN heterojunction photocatalyst was developed via decorating BQ on the surface of FCN. Benefiting from the collective effect of incorporated BQ, unique 3D rose flower-like structure as well as the 0D–3D heterojunction, the excitons were effectively dissociated and accelerated the separation of photogenerated carriers. Meanwhile, the visible light harvest range was broadened. The anchoring of BQ in FCN was verified by XRD, TEM, XPS, and so forth. The important role of induced BQ on the exciton dissociation and charge transfer behaviors were adequately illustrated by PL, TRPL and photoelectrochemical characterization techniques. The obtained BQ-FCN10 displays excellent photocatalytic activity in H2 evolution and removing colored dye RhB under the visible light irradiation, which is 17 and 3.71 times higher with respect to CN, respectively. On balance, this work provides a promising route to construct and regulate the exciton behavior in other polymeric photocatalysts for further applications.

Ke Cheng: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft; Weifan Shao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation; Haitong Li: Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation; Wen Guo: Formal analysis, Data curation; Huiyang Bian: Data curation, Formal analysis; Jiangang Han: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation; Guangyu Wu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation; Weinan Xing: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing- review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (21901119).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2022.116086.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
